stem: In a university admissions study comparing Department A vs. Department B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher acceptance rate (64.6% vs 57.1%). However, when stratified by applicant pool competitiveness: In less competitive applicants (n_X=40, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 78% vs Treatment Y's 71%. In highly competitive applicants (n_X=100, n_Y=30): Treatment X achieves 49% vs Treatment Y's 43%. The true population proportion of less competitive applicants is uncertain, estimated between 34% and 51%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to highly competitive applicants (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.064, 0.065], point estimate: 0.065. Treatment X is genuinely better by 6.5 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=57.1%, Y=64.6% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In less competitive applicants: X=78% > Y=71%. In highly competitive applicants: X=49% > Y=43%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Department A is applied more to highly competitive applicants (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
